sentences.text: I had a letter back.
My mother wrote in Russian.
My, my father wrote in Yiddish, and I learned Jewish-- Yiddish just now, through my husband.
And I didn't know well how to read, so I went to a friend.
My father wrote that if I met a guy and I'm going to be with him, he is his son.
My mother wrote to me that she always knew that a Russian woman, you could buy for three pair of silk stockings.
There is a book by Panteleimon Romanov, But she said that I sold myself, so to call-- in her imagination-- to survive.
She can understand.
But I sold my soul and went out of my class because I will never leave him.
And I couldn't say to her, mama, you are right, but I am not sad.
I build my life around this man, and it's a good life.
No.
I had-- I married to this man five times, each time in a different country, and if I would ever have to get a divorce, forget it.
[LAUGHS] The, the Jewish wedding I had while I was pregnant with Anita, with my older daughter, in America.
I went to a rabbi because after the war I met my nanna, who is not Jewish.
She raised me.
Mamma was busy, so I had a nanny.
And she took out the ring-- and she was not Jewish.
She wanted to take me, but the first thing the Nazis would look would be in her place, so she couldn't take my sister and I.

So she said, you are Jewish, and you owe your parents-- no matter you didn't have a religious home-- but you owe it to your people, the first chance you have in whichever country you come, you make sure that your children aren't born out of wedlock.
And with this ring, I got married.
In the forest was enough to come to the commander and say, comrade commander, I am going to Rubizhewsky.
And he said, OK.
If you have children, I do not tell you what to do, but the echo carries the cry of a child and it's hard to feed a child.
I am not telling you to have an abortion, but you must know that you will be sent to a group of women and children who are fed less and cannot fend for themselves.
And you yourself are responsible for that child.
There were no, no way were there any-- any-- anything to prevent a pregnancy like you have it nowadays, and women had abortions.
I had four.
I'm lucky I have the two girls.
And what I knew about sex or my husband, believe me, we didn't read it in a manual.
And we didn't know anything.
We were two kids.
He was 20.
I was 18.
And it's not like 20 and 18 nowadays.
I mean, when I-- sometimes ask my husband, did you have a look at another woman?
He said, did my father look?
Whatever.
I had my wife.
Why I should look at another woman?
Just never entered his mind.

And to this kind of a man, as difficult a man as he is because he is the Rock of Gibraltar-- I used to say during the war, when we went sometimes on a-- on a mission to do something, I used to say, give me a mule.
They said, what, a mule?
I said, to fight this mule.
Because he was stubborn.
But thanks to him, I had the rifle.
I had a gun.
I had a horse.
Because you had a man?
Because I had a man.
There is the answer.
And I don't care how a woman is liberated, and how she raises the child herself.
And when the child asks, where is my father?
And the child will want the father.
It's hard for me to understand because I used to say to my two daughters, your father is the king.
That makes me the queen.
That makes you two princesses.
Isn't it lovely?
To this day none of them, when we sit down in this beautiful dining room, picks up a spoon until the king of the family picks it up.
And somehow-- and my mother used to say another thing.
One soldier can do very little in the field.
But when you are the adjutant you can turn the head of the soldier.
So you have to maneuver somehow, and it has worked for me.
I had possibilities to go for an education further and get a degree.
And it was always in back of my mind, would my marriage be enough if I would get that degree?
No.
I owe my children stability.
And this man is the man of my choice, and with this man I stick till the end of my days.
And there was no other way, and that was it.
But partisans and fighting-- I resent that we haven't been recognized because there is nothing like a Jewish fighter.
First of all, you never leave the body of your fellow man.
I don't care.
I have a lot in common with American GIs.
You carry them because they are yours.
None of us-- there was one unsung hero.
Lowa Balkin was caught by the Nazis.
They tore his nails.
They gouged his eyes.
They hung him in the middle of the marketplace and said, that's what partisans look like, and he never told them where the bunkers are.
They beat me, and I didn't sing.
What am I, a hero?
No.
A Jew, we don't know what strength we have inside of us.
I knew I would die anyway.
I wanted to die with dignity.
Why give out all the connections of the righteous Christians?
They should die?
Or tell the police where my commander is.
I knew where he was.
When I came and I said, when you heard-- Tuvia, when you heard that I was caught, how come you didn't move?
He said, I knew which roots you came from.
You would not tell them anything.
There was-- I feel such a deep love for my people, and I buried my, my commander yesterday, Zosia.
To you all, he lived a full life.
He was a hero.
He was 83.
He had three sons.
He procreated.
I lost a brother, and I ache.
The love for, for-- we are-- we have nobody.
The children are here.
They have us, but we, we have each other.
And when a partisan meets a partisan-- you see, in the concentration camp, I don't blame them.
If I ate your potato, I lived longer.
In a army, if I shared my potato with you, we both live longer.
You die one for another.
And it's-- I am proud that I was a partisan.
My horse gave her life for me, my [?
Galla. ?] Her name was [?
Galla. ?] The last day we had an order that the Nazis are running through the woods not to let them through.
I don't know, I wasn't the leader.
You know, they give you-- if you had a little education, they made a lieutenant out of you.
You were an officer.
If you knew how to write, you were an officer.
And this was a hill, and we were 10 guys with one girl, and in the middle was a machine gun.
And they were running towards us, and everybody was dead and I was in the middle.
And I was shooting and shooting.
The last face I will remember till the day I die, that was a face of a Nazi not much more-- not, not much older than I-- must have been 19, with the blonde hair, running.
And then I saw blood, means I killed him.
He was shooting and I was shooting.
And in that minute, my horse came running from this little stable I had and covered me with her body, and got a bullet in her belly.
And Galla was-- horses, when they die, they cry, just like [INAUDIBLE] now.
If I can cry after a horse, I'm still human, right?
And she was dying, and I cannot shoot her.
A bullet in the stomach, forget it.
You cannot operate on a horse.
My husband was told that this hill-- next day, you are going to the city already.
We were liberated by the-- by the Russians.
It was 1944-- summer of 1944.
Next thing, my husband came.
They told them that there is a hill, and Sulia is there among all the dead.
He was coming for the body.
And he came, and he finds me in a daze.
He touches me all over-- I mean, blood.
He goes and touches every one of the guys-- dead. 10, all of them dead.
He shoots the horse, takes me by the hand-- and I don't know how I left that-- stepped over the bodies, and we left the hill.
We buried, later on, all the bodies.
And that last day, my girlfriend [?
Hannah ?] [?
Mader-- ?] she was grazed, but she died from a heart attack.
We buried her.
And then it was such a rain, like you had now in Bermuda-- what was it, Felix?
And in that rain, we were going home with the horses.
And the commander said, when you come before the town, I want you to walk in like soldiers.
Heads up.
Guns up.
And you go in and you sing in Jewish, and you sing the partisan song, mir zaynen do.
We are here, and we survived.
Eight years ago, Tuvia died.
Fridia ?] Zosia died.
And we are a dying generation, but we survived.
My husband said he's not going to Israel.
I wanted to.
I said, when I meet the first Russian, I will kiss his boots.
I did.
Could not stand the regime.
They wanted me to join the party.
I couldn't.
They took all the partisans-- they hated us-- and sent them to the front.
Oh my god.
The Russians sent the partisans.
They hated our guts because we were rebels.
They didn't need any rebels.
And my husband was an adjutant-- he'll tell you himself-- of a Russian.
We had one Russian commissar who called us my Jews.
It was supposed to be a compliment.
My Jews, he said-- the whole Jewish family.
He was a parachutist from Moscow, [NON-ENGLISH].
And the Russians sent him out from the Russian company, said, take your kikes and go with them.
You want them?
And he came to us.
He said, they made me a Jew, so I'm a Jew.
And my husband was his adjutant, his right hand.
And he, he said, nobody [INAUDIBLE] Boris, he said, nobody's going to take you to the front.
You are mine.
You know what it means to come to the town and stand in front of where our house was?
It wasn't there any more.
And in the middle, where our piano stood, there is-- we have a film done in England, and there is-- they made it there.
And there is the monument of a soldier with a girl, with the-- like in Arlington for JFK, they gave us an eternal light in the middle of my room there.
So as much as I disliked the regime, I am in awe of the Russian soldier.
They can take a dry piece of bread, and urinate on it and eat it and survive, and take out the, the harmoshka-- the-- the accordion and sing.
That's what they are, strong material.
And they helped us.
No matter what, how anti-Semitic they are, they helped the partisans.
Because if the English-- if Churchill and Roosevelt had bombed the rails, there wouldn't be any concentration camps.
There wouldn't be any ovens.
They didn't build them near us.
They had graves, but when we knew about the raid, we raided them.
They never came to that raid.
There wasn't a rail left.
There wasn't a bridge left.
We built our own, you know, those, from, from wood to go over the river.
We had the river Niemen there.
But every bridge was burnt.
All the partisans did, of course.
Sure.
With the Russians, together.
And when they had to come to us we were forewarned by the Nazi guards because they knew anyway.
They used to talk to each other, because they knew that some other people will come.
Usually, they were soldiers.
The worst were the SS and the brown-- the black and the brown shirts.
They did the whole thing.
They enjoyed it.
But most of the army were mobilized people.
And like mobilized people-- like an army-- they see atrocities, but they do it because they are given an order, so they do it.
But they were smart.
They never did it with their own.
They brought the Lithuanians and the Latvians and let them do it.
It was their orders, but the Latvians and the Lithuanians, the Ukrainians did it.
The Poles, they didn't because they killed the Poles too.
Anyway, it's not easy to be a Jew, but we had to run away from Russia on different names.
As former Polish citizens we were-- we were allowed to emigrate, but how can you emigrate?
My husband couldn't because [NON-ENGLISH], the comissar, got him a job like that from which he couldn't travel because then he is a traitor and he would be shot.
So we made ourselves false papers, and I took the general who helped my mother, I took his name.
So he was Aaron Kovelski and I was Stella Kovelski or Kovelska.
And with those names, we came to Poland.
And from Poland, we smuggled the border to Germany.
We came to the Russian zone.
From the Russian zone, we smuggled the border again to come to the Americans, and we came and registered in the American camp-- UNRRA camp-- Schlachtensee in Berlin.
And I'll tell you the truth that if not Truman, we would never come to the States.
Of course.
You smuggled.
If I would be caught, I would be shot.
You smuggled.
The Russians would shoot you.
Many were sent to Siberia and died there who were caught, some of our friends.
So that's why I say, [NON-ENGLISH].
It was meant to be there.
There must be something stronger, because not everything is because I took chances to survive.
And from Berlin, we registered, and we waited a year and a half to come to the States.
And to this day, I resent-- I am a citizen here.
I came here 48 years ago.
I am a citizen 43 years, and I'm proud of it.
And I resent that people come here, ask for asylum, and they are let in.
I had to be three weeks quarantined.

Nobody looked what kind of a psychological-- not on me, but on the others, because women were separated from men in Bremerhaven, which, when we were going out from, from to the States, and given showers.
People from the concentration camps thought they were being gassed, and they were screaming.
And they couldn't talk any-- speak any English.
Nobody could ask for an asylum.
And we knew that when we come to this country, we have to learn English.
I don't care how you spoke-- broken, not broken, with an accent, without an accent-- and this country doesn't owe you a thing.
It let you in.
You'll work yourself up from the boots up.
I never took from this country anything.
I don't want it.
I owe it.
You have to give back something.
And I resent when people HIV positive are let in.
We haven't got a cure for it.
Why was I kept three weeks, and they gave me all kinds of powder against lice-- which I didn't have-- [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE].
We were kept three weeks.
If you had a spot on your lung, you weren't going because maybe you had TB.
And you had already, I think-- no, the cure for TB came later, came in '49, came the Isoniazid that they found here.
But people were kept away, and the Statue of Liberty meant something.
You just didn't come in and take things for granted.
I was in Schlachtensee.
Only Schlachtensee in, in-- and we were useful.
I earned my ticket.
They didn't pay me money, I worked, and my husband worked.
They gave us food.
They gave us a room.
But we come from a culture that you don't take for nothing because we weren't beggars.
What do you mean, I'll come and they'll give me?
I'll come and I'll take?
Of course I could register someplace, and you only think I took.
I went to a clinic, and I told them how much money my husband makes, and that's how much I paid in the Bronx.
But I didn't take anything for free.
And anybody who comes now to my house can say, yes, you live in a palace.
Right.
Nobody gave it to me.
We earned it.
And if I wouldn't earn it, [INAUDIBLE] all of my people are a success.
Even if they live in small houses, small apartments, they raise their children.
They educated them.
They tried their best.
They didn't cop out.
I could have become an alcoholic.
I could have been on drugs.
I have an excuse.
How long will we go on blaming somebody for what we do?
So weaknesses-- and when I see what's going on in this country I ache because it's mine.
Did you think of going to Palestine?
Yes.
I go there visiting all the time.
My husband didn't want.
No, I mean before, at the end of the war.
I wanted to.
I wanted to.
I said, George, we sat in the woods all the time.
Let's go and fight for something of our own.
And he said, the war is ended.
I want to build a life without any wars.
I had enough.
I am the only one of my old brothers-- they were six brothers and three sisters.
He didn't know that his brother in Russia is alive, or his sister.
We weren't in touch yet.
One sister, one brother.
His sister is dead.
He said, I am going to America.
I said, do you have anybody there?
He said, if I have, I'll find them.
Who needs them?
I'll build my own way.
I said, I have family there.
He said, you want to?
Your right.
So this was how we came to the States.
We came to the States January the 24th, 1947, and Anita was born in America.
You came by ship?
By ship, the worst one you could ever think.
Oh, God.
Half of the ship was throwing up.
We left January the 7th, we came January the 24th.
Even the Marines were sick there.
The staff, everybody was throwing up.
I was in the hospital.
One woman died next to me, was buried at sea.
And then I saw people meeting as with flowers.
I said, isn't it wonderful?
I got well as soon as the Statue of Liberty was there, and I felt the ground.
I came up.
Wonderful.
They didn't need us.
They met Smetona, the president from, from Lithuania, the killer.
The Lithuanians.
Nobody met us, not nobody.
There wasn't a reporter, nobody.
And I said, nobody knows about-- there was so many millions killed-- nobody knows about us?
What's going on?
And then I started-- it started out, why did you go like sheep?
I said, what kind of a sheep?
What do you mean, we went to death?
My husband wasn't locked up.
What, are you any better?
Where were you?
Why didn't you protest?
Jews were being killed.
Why did you allow it?
I mean, the questions-- from Jewish people.
And one of my aunts said to me-- may she rest in peace-- why did you come here for, from Russia?
Why didn't you build socialism there?
I said, because I wanted to come someplace where it's free.
And why don't you take your bank book and go and build socialism in Moscow now.
Why do you sit here and tell me to stay there?
That I heard too.
But nobody gave us a penny, thank God.
Nobody can say that they helped us, thank God.
Whatever we have is ours.
Yeah.
She was in a dresser drawer.
I didn't have a crib.
I was told that I cannot have any children.
So after I came from the hospital and I could not have any children, they said some kind of a story that I had to have tubes blown, this blown.
I don't know what was blown, I became pregnant with my Anita without any blowing.
And I came to the States, and she's named after my mother and my sister.
And I put her in a dresser drawer.
I lived in the Bronx, in a furnished room.
And I went to the hospital-- first I went to the hospital, to the clinic.
And every time there was a different-- a different doctor examined you, and I was extremely afraid of it every time.
I called nurses sister, and she used to tell me, I'm not your sister.
And but after a few months I had a very limited vocabulary, but I understood everything and I spoke.
So evidently I had some kind of a ability to languages.
And then I said, I'm not going any more in the clinic.
I don't care if I have the baby in the street.
And I was sitting in the Bronx in Crotona Park, and I had two papers with me.
One was the PM, and the other one was a Jewish paper.
It took me long to read it.
And I was sitting there and said, what am I going to do?
And a man passed me by and he says to me in Yiddish, are you a refugee?
I said, yes.
And he says, I am a former GI.
I was a liberator of Dachau.
I said, right.
We started talking.
I said, and what do you do?
He says, I am a doctor, and said, what are you?
I said, and I am pregnant.
And he says to me, my name is Dr.
Fand, and I have an office right here not far from Prospect Avenue.
I said, yeah?
He said, are you registered in a hospital?
I said, no.
He said, then where will you have the baby?
I said, I don't know.
He said, in America you have to register.
Do you have $20 to register in the Bronx Hospital?
I will deliver your baby.
I said, I have to pay.
He said, OK.
You'll pay me when you have it.
I said, I have to know how much.
So I don't know, he said $50 or $60.
I said, I will pay you.
He said, I'm certain, but I'm going to deliver that baby.
And my husband had a camera, a Leica.
He sold it, and I had my baby.
Brought her and put her in a dresser drawer.
And you were living in a--
On Bryant Avenue in the Bronx, in a furnished room.
And my husband was a delivery in a grocery-- delivery boy.
I couldn't go to the HIAS because my aunt came and signed her name.
She came to the-- to the boat and signed her name, so HIAS wouldn't help me.
They said, you have relatives.
She wouldn't do anything.
It was my father's sister.
And they wouldn't give me-- and I wouldn't take from the HIAS either.
We weren't that-- my husband used to say, don't give me any, any money.
I'm not a beggar.
Give me a job.
We were just not that kind of people, so we never went to any organizations either.
No.
In the beginning I stayed with her, but that's when she told me the Jewish paper, I cannot bring in because it's not a leftist.
I have to bring in the not And then my uncle said to us that Jews like you should have been where the six million.
I didn't know that they were party members.
That's another story.
In America, it's very easy to be a communist.
They were leftists.
